content:
3 min read
Just now

--

It’s free, it’s online, and this year’s lineup is a surprisingly good read on where JavaScript is heading.
There’s no shortage of things competing for a developer’s attention, and free online conferences are especially easy to bookmark and then quietly forget. But a few earn the calendar slot. JS Days 2026 (September 16–17, online, free) is one I’d argue is worth it.
Quick disclosure before the pitch: I’m on the team at Sencha that helps run JS Days, so take my enthusiasm with that context. Here are five honest reasons it deserves two afternoons of your time.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. It’s free, online, and low-commitment
No ticket, no flights, no hotel, no manager sign-off. You join from your desk and drop into the sessions that matter to you. The barrier to entry is basically “have a browser and two afternoons free,” which makes it one of the easiest professional-development wins on the calendar this year.
2. The agenda is a pulse check on JavaScript in 2026
Even if you never attend, skimming the lineup tells you where the ecosystem is moving. The themes map almost exactly onto the conversations happening in every engineering channel right now: AI in the development workflow, modern front-end frameworks, UI frameworks and component libraries, enterprise-grade applications, and low-code and rapid application development. Two days of that is a fast way to take the temperature of the whole field.
3. AI-powered development gets a real, hands-on treatment
This is where a lot of conferences hand-wave, and where JS Days actually digs in. Instead of “look, it autocompletes a function,” there are sessions on building custom AI agents in JavaScript and React, generating whole applications on top of an existing database with AI, and building AI-powered UI components. The interesting throughline is the shift in posture: from AI writing snippets to AI assembling working systems that you steer. Whether you’re excited or skeptical, it’s worth hearing from people who’ve actually shipped it.
4. It goes deep on enterprise-grade JavaScript (the stuff that pays the bills)
Most events chase the shiny new framework. JS Days spends real time on the unglamorous, high-value work: handling large datasets, taming data grids (including the classic 100k-row React data grid problem), and keeping big applications fast and maintainable for years. Several of these sessions are built on Sencha Ext JS, the framework made for enterprise application development at scale, so you get concrete techniques rather than theory. If your day job involves real users and real data volumes, this track alone is worth the registration.
5. It’s live, so you can actually talk to the people building this
The sessions are live, which means real-time Q&A with Sencha’s team, MVPs, and independent developers. That back-and-forth (the “wait, why would you do it that way?” moment) is the part a recording never replaces. For two days, some of the people building these tools are a chat box away.
Put it on the calendar
If you write JavaScript, work on UI or enterprise applications, or you’re just trying to figure out where AI sensibly fits into your workflow, JS Days 2026 is an easy yes. It’s free, it’s online, and it runs September 16–17.
Registration is open now: jsdays.io
